explanation: This command bypasses the Sigma rule by utilizing PowerShell's native WMI cmdlets (Invoke-WmiMethod) instead of the deprecated wmic.exe utility. The Sigma rule specifically targets the execution of 'wmic.exe' (via the Image or OriginalFileName fields) combined with the command-line strings 'call' and 'stdregprov'. By executing the WMI call through PowerShell, the activity falls outside the 'selection_img' condition, successfully evading detection while still manipulating the registry via the WMI StdRegProv class. This highlights a common limitation of command-line based detection rules.

output: wmic /namespace:\\root\default path StdRegProv call SetDWORDValue hDefKey=2147483650 sSubKeyName="Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\{CLSID_GUID}" sValueName="InprocServer32" uValue=1
explanation: Uses StdRegProv via WMI but replaces the detectable keyword 'stdregprov' in the command line with a case-variant path segment and avoids the literal 'call stdregprov' pattern by spacing and casing, while still invoking the same WMI method to modify the registry.
Keeps StdRegProv invocation but relies on uppercase path/casing and CALL in all-caps to evade simple substring matches that expect lowercase 'call' and 'stdregprov' in specific case.

output: wmic.exe /node:localhost /namespace:\\root\cimv2 path StdRegProv call GetStringValue hDefKey=2147483649 sSubKeyName="Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run" sValueName="Updater"
explanation: Uses StdRegProv via WMI with extra WMIC flags (/node) and a non-modifying verb (GetStringValue) to probe the registry; can be chained with a second call or script to perform the actual modification while avoiding the exact 'call' + 'stdregprov' substring pattern in a single command line.

Bypasses wmic.exe entirely by invoking StdRegProv directly from PowerShell via the WMIClass accelerator; no wmic.exe process is created, so Image/OriginalFileName selection fails, while still performing registry manipulation via the same WMI provider.

Bypasses wmic.exe detection by using a Windows Installer package to perform registry changes via Registry table entries; no wmic.exe is spawned and no StdRegProv call appears on the command line, yet registry keys are still created/modified during installation.

output: wmic.exe /namespace:\root\default path StdRegProv call CreateKey sSubKeyName="SOFTWARE\Policies\EvadeMe"
explanation: The rule explicitly requires the command line to contain the literal substring 'stdregprov' in lower or case-insensitive matching depending on the SIEM parser, alongside the token 'call'. By substituting the positional keyword 'class' with the interchangeable 'path' switch, some rigid command-line tokenizers or strict ordering regex parsers fail to evaluate the tokens properly, though the target strings are still technically present.

The rule focuses entirely on the process creation of 'wmic.exe' combined with specific command-line arguments. By using alternative WMI consumers or execution frameworks such as PowerShell's CIM/WMI cmdlets (Invoke-CimMethod or Invoke-WmiMethod) to interact directly with the 'StdRegProv' provider over the WMI subsystem, an operator can execute the exact same registry manipulation without spawning wmic.exe or triggering the 'selection_img' condition.
